examples.title: Basic Backward Chaining Example
examples.description: Simple Prolog program demonstrating backward chaining for disease diagnosis
examples.code:
% Knowledge Base
has_fever(john).
has_cough(john).
has_fatigue(john).

% Rules (Horn Clauses)
has_flu(X) :- has_fever(X), has_cough(X), has_fatigue(X).
has_cold(X) :- has_cough(X), has_sneeze(X).

% Query
% ?- has_flu(john).
% Prolog proves by backward chaining: starts with goal has_flu(john),
% matches rule, proves sub-goals has_fever(john), has_cough(john), has_fatigue(john)
examples.explanation: This Prolog code shows backward chaining: system starts with goal has_flu(john), finds matching rule, then recursively proves sub-goals using known facts. Uses DFS to pursue each branch.

examples.title: General Backward Chaining Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudocode for the backward chaining inference engine
examples.code:
FUNCTION Backward_Chain(goal):
 IF goal is already proved THEN
 RETURN true with bindings
 
 IF goal is a fact THEN
 RETURN match with fact
 
 FOR EACH rule R where R.head matches goal:
 Push sub-goals of R.body onto goal stack
 
 WHILE goal stack is not empty:
 current_goal = Pop from stack
 
 IF NOT Backward_Chain(current_goal):
 // Failed, backtrack
 Unbind variables
 Restore previous stack state
 CONTINUE to next rule
 
 // All sub-goals proved
 RETURN true with bindings
 
 RETURN false // No rule matched
examples.explanation: The algorithm uses recursive goal reduction with backtracking. It uses a goal stack (LIFO), depth-first search, and backtracks when any sub-goal fails by trying alternative rules.

examples.title: Demonstrating Backtracking
examples.description: Prolog program showing how backtracking finds multiple solutions
examples.code:
% Knowledge Base with multiple facts
parent(tom, mary).
parent(tom, james).
parent(tom, anna).
parent(mary, susan).

grandparent(X, Y) :- parent(X, Z), parent(Z, Y).

% Query: ?- grandparent(tom, Who).
% 
% Backward chaining trace:
% 1. Goal: grandparent(tom, Who)
% 2. Match rule: grandparent(X,Y) :- parent(X,Z), parent(Z,Y)
% 3. First, parent(tom, Z) → Z=mary (first fact)
% 4. Then parent(mary, Who) → Who=susan
% 5. Returns Who=susan
% 6. On semicolon (;) input, backtracks:
% - Unbinds Who
% - Tries next parent(tom, Z) → Z=janna (second fact)
% - Returns no match
% 7. Backtracks further: parent(tom, Z) → Z=anna
% - Returns no match
% 8. All alternatives exhausted → false
examples.explanation: Shows backtracking in action: Prolog tries one path, finds solution, then on user prompt tries alternatives. Backtracking undoes variable bindings and explores new paths.
